candidates.title: Unified Dynamic Weight Decay: A Proximal-Theoretic Framework with Standardized Evaluation and Comprehensive Benchmarking

collisions.paper: Chen et al., 2025. Cautious Weight Decay. arXiv:2510.12402, ICLR 2026
collisions.overlap: CWD proposes sign-alignment-based selective weight decay (WD applied only when sign(w) aligns with optimizer update). The proposal's Phi-framework encompasses CWD as a special case (directional axis). CWD also includes ablation with random mask and gradient-based mask. However, CWD does NOT provide a unified framework for all four WD sub-approaches, does not propose standardized metrics, and does not include compute-controlled benchmarks against SWD/AdamWN/cosine-WD.
collisions.differentiation: CWD is one method within the proposed taxonomy. The proposal's contribution is the unification, not CWD itself. However, CWD's random-mask ablation partially pre-empts the CWD falsification battery (though it did NOT test effective-lambda matching, inverted mask, or continuous modulation).

collisions.paper: D'Angelo et al., 2024. Why Do We Need Weight Decay in Modern Deep Learning? NeurIPS 2024
collisions.overlap: Establishes that WD modifies effective learning rate rather than acting as classical regularizer. Shows WD primarily serves optimization, not generalization. This aligns with the proposal's H6 (spatial > temporal via effective LR redistribution).
collisions.differentiation: D'Angelo studies WD mechanisms but does NOT compare dynamic WD methods, does not propose the Phi-framework or proximal unification, and does not provide standardized metrics or comprehensive benchmarks.

collisions.paper: Xie et al., 2020/2023. On the Overlooked Pitfalls of Weight Decay / Scheduled Weight Decay (SWD). NeurIPS 2023, arXiv:2011.11152
collisions.overlap: Proposes SWD (gradient-norm-aware WD scheduler). Addresses temporal WD scheduling. Shows Adam+SWD outperforms AdamW on CIFAR.
collisions.differentiation: SWD addresses only the temporal axis. The proposal encompasses SWD as one case within the Phi-framework. SWD does not address alignment-aware, norm-matched, or spatial modulation. No head-to-head comparison with CWD or AdamWN.

collisions.paper: Loshchilov, 2023. Weight Norm Control / AdamWN. arXiv:2311.11446
collisions.overlap: Proposes AdamWN that generalizes AdamW by setting a non-zero target norm. Shows AdamW is a special case where target norm = 0.
collisions.differentiation: AdamWN addresses only the target-norm axis. The proposal encompasses AdamWN as one case. AdamWN does not provide a unified framework, does not benchmark against CWD/SWD, and does not propose evaluation metrics.

collisions.paper: Xie & Li, 2024. Implicit Bias of AdamW: L-infinity Norm Constrained Optimization. arXiv:2404.04454
collisions.overlap: Shows AdamW implicitly performs L-infinity constrained optimization via connection between normalized steepest descent + weight decay and Frank-Wolfe. Proximal interpretation of Adam/WD.
collisions.differentiation: Focuses on AdamW's implicit bias, not on dynamic/time-varying WD. Does not address CWD, SWD, or AdamWN. Does not propose the four-axis taxonomy or standardized metrics.

collisions.paper: Ding et al., 2023. Adam-family Methods with Decoupled Weight Decay. arXiv:2310.08858
collisions.overlap: Proposes a general framework for Adam-family methods with decoupled weight decay. Proves convergence for non-diminishing stepsizes.
collisions.differentiation: Focuses on convergence theory for fixed WD, not time-varying/dynamic WD. Does not address CWD/SWD/AdamWN or the four-axis taxonomy.

collisions.paper: Kosson et al., 2023. Rotational Equilibrium: How Weight Decay Balances Learning Across Neural Networks. arXiv:2305.17212
collisions.overlap: Studies how WD affects per-neuron rotation rates, effective learning rate balancing. Shows WD creates 'rotational equilibrium' across layers.
collisions.differentiation: Studies WD mechanisms (consistent with the proposal's effective-LR narrative) but does not address dynamic WD methods or propose a unified framework.

collisions.paper: Wan et al., 2020. Spherical Motion Dynamics. arXiv:2006.08419
collisions.overlap: Comprehensively studies learning dynamics with normalization + WD + SGD. Proves weight norm converges at linear rate. Proposes 'angular update' as substitute for effective learning rate.
collisions.differentiation: Studies dynamics of fixed WD with normalization, not dynamic WD variants. Relevant background but does not compete with the proposal's contribution.

collisions.paper: Yun et al., 2020. A General Family of Stochastic Proximal Gradient Methods for Deep Learning. arXiv:2007.07484
collisions.overlap: Proposes unified proximal gradient framework encompassing L1 regularization, group sparsity, etc. via proximal operators. Considers decoupled weight decay.
collisions.differentiation: Proximal framework for general regularizers, not specifically for dynamic/time-varying WD methods. Does not address CWD, SWD, AdamWN, or the four modulation axes.

collisions.paper: Newhouse, 2025. Duality, Weight Decay, and Metrized Deep Learning. MIT Thesis
collisions.overlap: Explores duality frameworks connecting optimizers (SGD, Adam, Shampoo) with WD and norm-based regularization. Connects WD to mirror descent.
collisions.differentiation: Thesis-level treatment of WD duality. Does not derive the four-way proximal unification or propose standardized evaluation metrics.

collisions.paper: Franke et al., 2024. Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR). NeurIPS 2024
collisions.overlap: Proposes per-parameter adaptive regularization as alternative to WD. Uses upper bounds on statistical measures of individual parameter matrices with Lagrange multipliers.
collisions.differentiation: CPR is a new method, not a unification of existing ones. Does not provide taxonomy of existing WD variants. Relevant as additional baseline/comparison but does not subsume the proposal's contributions.

collisions.paper: Guo & Fan, 2025. AdamHD: Decoupled Huber Decay Regularization. arXiv:2511.14721
collisions.overlap: Replaces L2 penalty in WD with Huber regularizer. Uses proximal update formulation.
collisions.differentiation: Proposes one new WD variant (Huber). Does not unify existing methods or provide comprehensive benchmarks.

collisions.paper: Blake et al., 2025. Weight Decay May Matter More Than muP. arXiv:2510.19093
collisions.overlap: Builds unifying framework for WD and muP based on 'relative updates'. Shows independent WD overrides muP scaling.
collisions.differentiation: Focuses on WD's interaction with muP for learning rate transfer, not on comparing dynamic WD variants. Different focus despite also building a 'unifying framework'.

collisions.paper: Caraffa, 2026. Thermodynamically Optimal Regularization under Information-Geometric Constraints. arXiv:2601.17330
collisions.overlap: Proposes Fisher-Rao metric as unique geometry for regularization. Shows classical regularization schemes (including WD) are structurally incapable of guaranteeing thermodynamic optimality.
collisions.differentiation: Theoretical framework from thermodynamic perspective. The proposal mentions Fisher-Rao WD as a future extension. Caraffa's work provides theoretical foundations but does not benchmark dynamic WD methods.

collisions.paper: Sadrtdinov et al., 2025. Can Training Dynamics Be Explained by Thermodynamics of an Ideal Gas? arXiv:2511.07308
collisions.overlap: Develops thermodynamic framework for SGD+WD dynamics on scale-invariant networks. Maps hyperparameters to thermodynamic variables.
collisions.differentiation: Studies fixed WD with normalization through thermodynamic lens. Does not address dynamic WD methods or provide benchmarks.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The unified Phi-framework organizing all four WD sub-approaches (temporal, directional, target, spatial) into a single per-parameter modulation function is novel. No existing work provides this complete taxonomy. The WD Stability Condition for time-varying regularizers is novel. The standardized metrics (BEM/CSI/AIS) have no precedent. The compute-controlled head-to-head benchmark of all major WD variants is novel. The CWD falsification battery extends beyond CWD's own ablations (adding effective-lambda matching, inverted mask, continuous modulation). Key risk: the 'unification as notation' criticism. Mitigate by proving composition theorems and the WD Stability Condition's predictive power.

candidates.title: Rethinking Dynamic Weight Decay: Mechanism Decomposition Reveals When and Why Temporal Scheduling Fails While Spatial Modulation Succeeds

collisions.paper: D'Angelo et al., 2024. Why Do We Need Weight Decay in Modern Deep Learning? NeurIPS 2024
collisions.overlap: Already establishes that WD primarily modulates effective learning rate, not regularization. The contrarian paper's core message (WD scheduling is about effective LR, not regularization) is substantially anticipated.
collisions.differentiation: D'Angelo does not specifically test dynamic WD methods or provide budget equivalence experiments. The contrarian paper's CWD falsification and head-to-head benchmark are novel.

collisions.paper: Chen et al., 2025. Cautious Weight Decay. ICLR 2026
collisions.overlap: CWD's random-mask ablation shows that random masking underperforms CWD, partially contradicting the hypothesis that alignment-awareness does not matter. The contrarian paper needs to address this carefully.
collisions.differentiation: CWD's ablation tested random mask vs. CWD but did NOT test effective-lambda-matched constant WD (the key contrarian experiment). The effective-lambda matching test is the novel contribution.

collisions.paper: Xie et al., 2023. SWD / On the Overlooked Pitfalls of Weight Decay. NeurIPS 2023
collisions.overlap: SWD claims temporal scheduling improves performance. The contrarian paper would argue this is budget-non-equivalent comparison. Need to address SWD's claims directly.
collisions.differentiation: SWD does not test budget equivalence (mean-matched constant WD). The contrarian paper's core contribution is exactly this controlled comparison.

collisions.paper: Van Laarhoven, 2017. L2 Regularization versus Batch and Weight Normalization. arXiv:1706.05350
collisions.overlap: Already shows WD with normalization primarily affects effective learning rate, not regularization.
collisions.differentiation: Foundational work. The contrarian paper builds on this insight but applies it specifically to dynamic WD methods.

collisions.paper: Kosson et al., 2023. Rotational Equilibrium. arXiv:2305.17212
collisions.overlap: Shows WD balances effective learning rates across layers through rotational equilibrium.
collisions.differentiation: Supports the contrarian narrative but does not test dynamic WD methods.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The pure contrarian-empirical approach faces higher publication risk because D'Angelo et al. (NeurIPS 2024) already substantially establishes the 'WD as effective LR' narrative. The novel contributions are: (1) budget equivalence testing across datasets/architectures, (2) CWD effective-lambda matching (not done by CWD authors), and (3) head-to-head benchmark. However, without the unified framework, the paper risks being perceived as incremental validation of D'Angelo's findings. Recommend pairing with the unified framework (cand_unified_framework) for stronger positioning.

candidates.title: Allostatic Weight Decay: A Multi-Timescale Regulation Framework Inspired by Synaptic Homeostasis and Thermodynamic Process Optimization

collisions.paper: Zenke et al., 2013. Synaptic Plasticity in Neural Networks Needs Homeostasis with a Fast Rate Detector. PLOS Comp Bio
collisions.overlap: Studies homeostasis + weight decay in spiking neural networks. Shows need for multi-timescale mechanisms.
collisions.differentiation: Computational neuroscience context (spiking networks). Has NOT been applied to deep learning WD. The allostatic WD idea is a novel transfer of this concept.

collisions.paper: Caraffa, 2026. Thermodynamically Optimal Regularization. arXiv:2601.17330
collisions.overlap: Connects thermodynamic optimality with regularization in deep learning via Fisher-Rao metric.
collisions.differentiation: Caraffa provides theoretical thermodynamic framework but does NOT propose multi-timescale adaptive WD or biologically-inspired hierarchy. The allostatic approach is complementary.

collisions.paper: Sadrtdinov et al., 2025. Thermodynamics of an Ideal Gas for Training Dynamics. arXiv:2511.07308
collisions.overlap: Develops ideal gas analogy for SGD+WD dynamics.
collisions.differentiation: Provides thermodynamic framework that could support the allostatic WD's Level 3 (predictive scheduling). Does NOT propose multi-timescale adaptive WD.

collisions.paper: Franke et al., 2024. CPR. NeurIPS 2024
collisions.overlap: CPR adapts regularization per-parameter over time using Lagrange multipliers.
collisions.differentiation: CPR is a single-mechanism approach. Allostatic WD proposes a three-level hierarchy (reactive + adaptive + predictive) which is fundamentally different.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Zero arXiv/Scholar results for 'allostatic weight decay' - this is genuinely novel framing. The multi-timescale hierarchy combining reactive (per-parameter alignment), adaptive (norm tracking), and predictive (anticipatory scheduling) has no precedent in deep learning WD literature. The periodic 'sleep phase' WD boost is unprecedented. However, high novelty comes with high risk: (1) many hyperparameters, (2) biological analogy may be superficial for ML reviewers, (3) improvement margins may be small. The thermodynamic vocabulary is now partially pre-empted by Caraffa (2026) and Sadrtdinov et al. (2025), though neither proposes an allostatic controller.
